coredata.dc:title: Surface passivated p-phenylenediamine carbon quantum dots (p-CQDs) as fluorescent turn-on probes for the detection of Li+ and L-arginine by two different mechanisms
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Qian
Pu, Zixuan
Wang, Ying
Li, Mingtian
coredata.dc:description: Surface modification technique is currently acknowledged as a practical, efficient, and acceptable tool for producing large quantities of high-quality carbon quantum dots (CQDs), however the research on the quenching and recovery mechanism of the fluorescence of CQDs should receive more attention. Herein, the p-phenylenediamine carbon quantum dots (p-CQDs) exhibiting outstanding characteristics has been synthesized from erbium nitrate (Er(NO3)3) ethanol solution. It shows as a highly efficient “on-off-on” fluorescent probe for the detection of Fe3+, Li+ and l-arginine (L-Arg). The fluorescence of p-CQDs can be quenched by the appearance of Fe3+ (p-CQDs-Fe3+), then can be restored by adding either Li+ or L-Arg (p-CQDs-Fe3+-Li+ or p-CQDs-Fe3+-L-Arg). The zeta potentials were measured for the four fluorescent systems p-CQDs, p-CQDs-Fe3+, p-CQDs-Fe3+-Li+ and p-CQDs-Fe3+-L-Arg. The results show that the loss of charges on the surface of CQDs is the key point which can determine this probe to remain “on” or “off” mode. In addition, we found that the mechanism of fluorescence “turn-on” is different in p-CQDs-Fe3+-Li+ and p-CQDs-Fe3+-L-Arg system, the former is trying to open the transmission channel of intramolecular photons, which manifests as fluorescence recovery. The latter may strip Fe3+ from the surface of p-CQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence turn-on probe
Fe3+
Li+
l-arginine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of Li + on the fluorescence of p-CQDs-Fe3+
Effect of amino acid on the fluorescence of p-CQDs-Fe3+

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Science and Technology Fund of Sichuan Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Sichuan University of Science and Engineering

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Innovation Fund of Postgraduate , Sichuan University of Science & Engineering (No. y2021006 and S202110622040 ), Science and Technology Fund of Sichuan Province (No. 2020ZHCG0081 ), for which all the authors are grateful.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images of p-CQDs; (B) High-resolution TEM images of p-CQDs; (C) TEM images of x-CQDs. Inset of A and C: the size distribution of p-CQDs and x-CQDs.
(A) XRD patterns of p-CQDs and x-CQDs; (B) FI-TR spectra of p-CQDs and x-CQDs.
(A) XPS spectra of p-CQDs; (B–D) High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, N1s and O1s for p-CQDs.

(A) UV–Vis absorption spectra of p-CQDs; (B) The fluorescence emission spectra of p-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths (310–410 nm); (C) UV–Vis absorption spectra of x-CQDs; (D) The fluorescence emission spectra of x-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths (420–560 nm). Insets of A and C: p-CQDs and x-CQDs solution taken in visible light (left) and 365 nm UV light (right).

(A) Overlay fluorescence spectra corresponding to the respective absorbance of QS and p-CQDs; (B) Slope diagram of QS and p-CQDs; (C) Overlay fluorescence spectra corresponding to the respective absorbance of RhB and x-CQDs; (D) Slope diagram of RhB and x-CQDs.

(A) Normalized fluorescence intensity of x-CQDs in the presence of 18 metal ions; (B) Fluorescence spectral changes of x-CQDs in the presence of 18 metal ions; (C) Fluorescence spectral changes of p-CQDs in the presence of 18 metal ions; (D) Normalized fluorescence intensity of p-CQDs in the presence of 18 metal ions; (E) Interference of the remaining metal ions with Fe3+ in p-CQDs solution; (F) Effect of 11 anions on the fluorescence intensity of p-CQDs. Inset of C: Comparison of p-CQDs solution (left) and p-CQDs-Fe3+ solution (right) at 365 nm.

(A) The fluorescence spectra of p-CQDs solutions after the addition of different concentrations of Fe3+ (from top to bottom: 0, 3, 6, 9, …, and 84 μM); (B) Linear relationship between F/F0 with the different concentration of Fe3+.

(A) Normalized histogram of the p-CQDs-Fe3+ system with the addition of various metal ions; (B) Normalized histogram of the interference of other 17 metal ions (blue bars) on the fluorescence intensity after the addition of Li+ (red bars) to the p-CQDs-Fe3+ solution (black bars); (C) Dependence of fluorescence spectra of p-CQDs-Fe3+ on Li + concentration in the range of 0–270 μM; (D) Linear relationship between F/F0 with the different concentration of Li+.

(A) Normalized histogram of the p-CQDs-Fe3+ system with the addition of various amino acids; (B) Normalized histogram of the interference of other 4 amino acids (green bars) on the fluorescence intensity after the addition of L-Arg (red bars) to the p-CQDs-Fe3+ solution (black bars); (C) Dependence of FL intensity of p-CQDs-Fe3+ on L-Arg concentration in the range of 0–125 μM; (D) Linear relationship between F/F0 with the different concentration of L-Arg.
Zeta potentials of p-CQDs, p-CQDs-Fe3+, p-CQDs-Fe3+-L-Arg and p-CQDs-Fe3+-Li+ solutions.
Comparison between the proposed method and other techniques for Fe3+ detection.
Comparison between the proposed method and other techniques for Li + detection.
Comparison between the proposed method and other techniques for L-Arg detection.

head.ce:title: Surface passivated p-phenylenediamine carbon quantum dots (p-CQDs) as fluorescent turn-on probes for the detection of Li+ and L-arginine by two different mechanisms

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Sichuan University of Science & Engineering, Zigong, 643000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Sichuan University of Science & Engineering, Zigong 643000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Material Corrosion and Protection Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, Zigong, 643000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Material Corrosion and Protection Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Material Corrosion and Protection Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, Zigong 643000, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Materials Science and Engineering, Sichuan University of Science & Engineering, Zigong, 643000, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Surface modification technique is currently acknowledged as a practical, efficient, and acceptable tool for producing large quantities of high-quality carbon quantum dots (CQDs), however the research on the quenching and recovery mechanism of the fluorescence of CQDs should receive more attention. Herein, the p-phenylenediamine carbon quantum dots (p-CQDs) exhibiting outstanding characteristics has been synthesized from erbium nitrate (Er(NO3)3) ethanol solution. It shows as a highly efficient “on-off-on” fluorescent probe for the detection of Fe3+, Li+ and l-arginine (L-Arg). The fluorescence of p-CQDs can be quenched by the appearance of Fe3+ (p-CQDs-Fe3+), then can be restored by adding either Li+ or L-Arg (p-CQDs-Fe3+-Li+ or p-CQDs-Fe3+-L-Arg). The zeta potentials were measured for the four fluorescent systems p-CQDs, p-CQDs-Fe3+, p-CQDs-Fe3+-Li+ and p-CQDs-Fe3+-L-Arg. The results show that the loss of charges on the surface of CQDs is the key point which can determine this probe to remain “on” or “off” mode. In addition, we found that the mechanism of fluorescence “turn-on” is different in p-CQDs-Fe3+-Li+ and p-CQDs-Fe3+-L-Arg system, the former is trying to open the transmission channel of intramolecular photons, which manifests as fluorescence recovery. The latter may strip Fe3+ from the surface of p-CQDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), showing excellent fluorescence effect, are carbon-based zero-dimensional materials with sizes less than 10 nm [1,2]. Due to the advantages of wide source of raw materials [3], low cost, excellent water solubility, environmental friendliness, and non-toxic [4], CQDs have broad application value in many fields, such as biochemical sensors, medical imaging, optoelectronic devices, and environmental detection [5]. Furthermore, the physical and chemical properties of CQDs can be readily modified and tuned by their surface, composition and morphology. The overall structure of the system or the Fermi energy level may change due to the increase of defects produced by hetero elements, thus increasing the electron activity in the conduction band [6]. As a result, the modification of CQDs has gained increased popularity and attention in the fields of nano-sensors and environmental monitoring [7]. Sun et al. synthesized Ni-doped CQDs that enhanced the detectability of metal ions, and achieved the goal of simultaneous detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+ by electrochemical method [8]. Based on the “on-off-on” system, Zhao et al. established a sensitive and selective fluorescence sensor for turn-off detection of Hg2+ ions and turn-on detection of cysteine [9]. Phenylenediamine, as an aromatic diamine, has reactive amino groups that can provide more remodification groups in the reaction, and there are off-domain large π bonds within the molecule. It has been reported to use three isomers of phenylenediamine to prepare CQDs with trichrome fluorescence for multicolor cell imaging [10]. It is reported that the preparation of near infrared carbon dots from o-phenylenediamine has been made in kilogram-scale with 96% yield [11]. Multicolor carbon dots were prepared from m-phenylenediamine and urea as multifunctional fluorescent probes [12]. Surface passivation of carboxylated carbon nanoparticles with p-phenylenediamine was used to successfully synthesize CQDs with stable excitation-independent green photoluminescence [13]. However, a majority of studies on CQDs prepared from various phenylenediamine isomers as carbon sources are immature in the detection of metal ions or amino acids.

Iron is one of the most abundant elements, also known as the transition metal in human body [14]. It serves as an essential industrial reagent and is required for the maintenance of certain physiological processes [15]. Fe3+ is also essential for biological processes such as oxygen transport, cellular metabolism, and enzymatic catalysis [16]. Traditional methods for Fe3+ detection, for instance, atomic absorption spectroscopy [17,18], electrochemical detection [19] and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [20]. Despite the high sensitivity of these techniques, they still have drawbacks such as high detection cost, cumbersome operation, unstable electrodes and complicated sample handling. In this regard, it remains a challenge to rapidly and affordably analyze drinking water for quantities of iron and other metal ion contaminants [14]. Lithium ions (Li+) are intercalated and deintercalated from positive electrode to negative electrode over and over again during the charging process of lithium-ion batteries [21]. Unfortunately, in some anomalous cases, Li⁺ precipitates on the surface of the negative electrode, leading to severe capacity loss and potential safety issues. Solid state nuclear magnetic resonance [22], Mass spectrometry [23], and Titration gas chromatography technologies are currently useful detection techniques for quantifying Li+ [24]. However, it is typically difficult to implement it on a broad scale in actual battery examination, due to issues including the high cost of testing equipment and challenging sample preparation. On the other hand, one of the most extensively utilized amino acids in mammalian cells is l-arginine (L-Arg) [25]. In addition to being a precursor to protein synthesis, it also stimulates the production of urea, catalyzes the cycle of ornithine, and converts ammonia in the body into non-toxic urea. In the fermentation business, if the raw material has an excessive amount of L-Arg, the urea that builds up in the fermentation broth may react with the ethanol to generate ethyl carbamate, which may have cancer-causing properties in the human body [26]. Consequently, the development of a straightforward, reliable, speedy, and accurate approach for detecting L-Arg content is crucial for both fundamental research and clinical diagnosis [27].

In our work, a new method to improve the physicochemical properties of CQDs was developed. A blue-fluorescent p-phenylenediamine carbon quantum dots (p-CQDs) was created by utilizing p-phenylenediamine as the carbon source and erbium nitrate (Er(NO3)3) in ethanol (fluorescence turn-on). It is worth mentioning that the CQDs made from the other nitrates, such as Ce(NO3)3, La(NO3)3, Pr(NO3)3 and Fe(NO3)3, have poor fluorescence performance. In a p-CQDs based chemosensor, three sensing systems for the detection of Fe3+, Li+, and L-Arg are combined. It has been reported that the fluorescence of CQDs can be turned off by energy and electron transfer. Since the abundant functional groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl, etc.) on the surface of p-CQDs can covalently interact with Fe3+, the original conjugation system of CQDs is disrupted [28,29], therefore, p-CQDs is used as a fluorescent “turn-off” chemical sensor for Fe3+ detection (p-CQDs-Fe3+). Additionally, the addition of Li+ or L-Arg to the p-CQDs-Fe3+ system could restore the fluorescence of the system (fluorescence on-off-on). The mechanisms for fluorescence restoration in the p-CQDs-Fe3+ system are proposed according to the results of zeta potential test. The former is that Li + ions open the transmission channel of intramolecular photons, which manifests as fluorescence “turn-on”. The latter is that L-Arg coordinates with Fe3+ stripping from the surface of p-CQDs. The discovered method is promising for both quantitative inorganic ion and amino acid detection.

p-Phenylenediamine, Er(NO3)3 and anhydrous alcohol from Chron Chemicals Co., Ltd (Chengdu, Sichuan). l-arginine (L-Arg), l-histidine (L-His), l-cysteine (L-Cys), l-valine (L-Val) and l-lysine (L-Lys) were analysis grade and purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). In this experiment, all metal ions used were nitrates acquired from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China), except for nickel chloride (NiCl2, 98%), ferric chloride (FeCl3, 98%), ferrous chloride (FeCl2, 98%), magnesium chloride (MgCl2, 98%) and mercury sulfate (HgSO4, 98%). All analytical reagents were obtained from commercial resources and used without further purification. Dialysis bags of different specifications were obtained from Yibo Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Hunan China). The ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm at 25 °C) was used throughout the experiments.

The p-CQDs were prepared by one-step solvothermal method using p-phenylenediamine as the carbon source and an ethanolic solution of Er(NO3)3 as the solvent [30]. The mixture was magnetically stirred for 20 min and then transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene reactor, and then being insulated at 200 °C for 10 h. After cooled down to room temperature, the obtained solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min to remove the insoluble material. The recovered supernatant was then dialyzed for 3 days against ultrapure water using a dialysis membrane (500 Da). The purified solution was freeze-dried into powder at −60 °C for 48 h, thus p-CQDs powder was obtained.
As a reference, carbon quantum dots (x-CQDs) were obtained using the same procedure for p-CQDs but absence of Er(NO3)3.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra were measured by UV-PC1800 UV–Vis Photometer (Mapada, China). The fluorescence spectra were recorded by FL-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan), and the emission peak area was calculated through the trend of spectral curve to obtain the relative fluorescence quantum yield of the sample. The size and morphology of the p-CQDs and x-CQDs were analyzed by transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (JEOL model JEM 2100, Japan). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were recorded using a Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement was detected by D2 PHASER (Bruker, Germany) with a voltage and current at 40 kV and 40 mA. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were carried out on the Nicolet iS10 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). Anton Paar (Litesizer) 500™ instrument (Anton Paar, Austria) for measuring zeta potential values to further explain the mechanism of fluorescence intensity changes in CQDs [31].

Fluorescence quantum yield of p-CQDs was determined with reference to quinine sulfate (QS) with quantum yield of 54% in 0.1 M H2SO4. The x-CQDs were determined with Rhodamine B (RhB) as standard (Ф = 92%) in anhydrous ethanol solution. The relative quantum yield was calculated according to the following formula [32]:
Ф X
=
Ф
S T
(
k X
/
k
S T
)
(
η X
/
η
S T
)
2
where Ф X and Ф ST are the quantum yield of the sample and the reference, respectively, k X and k ST represent the linear slope of the fluorescence integral area of the sample and the reference and their absorbances, respectively. η X, η ST are the refractive index of the sample to be measured and the reference, respectively.

At room temperature, 20 μL of Fe3+ (5.0 mM) aqueous solution was homogeneously mixed with 2 mL of p-CQDs (1.0 mg/mL) ethanolic solution, marked as p-CQDs-Fe3+. The fluorescence spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm and a constant excitation/emission slit width of 5 nm. The concentrations of the other metal ions (Hg2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ce3+, La3+, Li+, Ag+, Zn2+, Co2+, Na+, Mn2+, K+, Ni2+, Sn2+) and anions (H2PO4 −, HCO3 −, CO3 2−, Cl−, ClO4 −, NO2 −, NO3 −, OH−, SiO3 2−, SCN−, SO4 2−) added to the p-CQDs were all 5.0 mM. Then, the sensitivity for Fe3+ were investigated by different concentrations of Fe3+ (0–84 μM) in p-CQDs solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Li + on the fluorescence of p-CQDs-Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: Li+ was identified by introducing various Li+ concentrations (0–270 μM) to the p-CQDs-Fe3+ system and monitoring the intensity of the fluorescence. The fluorescence recovery of other metal ions on the p-CQDs-Fe3+ system was examined under the same experimental settings.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of amino acid on the fluorescence of p-CQDs-Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: L-Arg with different concentrations (0–125 μM) was added into the p-CQDs-Fe3+ system, and the fluorescence spectra were measured. Under comparable experimental settings, the interfering impact of several amino acids (L-Val, L-His, L-Cys and L-Lys) on L-Arg was investigated.

The p-CQDs and x-CQDs were synthesized by a simple solvothermal technique with ethanol as the reaction media. TEM, XRD, FT-IR, XPS, UV–Vis, and fluorescence spectra were then used to evaluate the product.

As shown in Fig. 1 A, p-CQDs particles are nearly spherical in shape with a diameter of 1.92 nm ± 0.4 nm. It can be seen from Fig. 1B, p-CQDs have a uniform dispersion and the lattice spacing is 0.291 nm. Fig. 1C shows that x-CQDs have an average particle size of 4.21 nm ± 0.6 nm and are easily agglomerated. It can be explained by the fact that p-phenylenediamine is an important precursor for the production of many heterocyclic compounds and polymers [10]. With the formation of C–N bonds in polymer fragments, x-CQDs prepared by p-phenylenediamine without surface passivation are prone to agglomeration [33]. On the contrary, the surface state or the number of surface functional groups of p-CQDs treated by erbium nitrate are changed, so the surface of p-CQDs is passivated and the particles are prone to disperse. Therefore, the dispersibility of p-CQDs is enhanced.

As shown in Fig. 2 A, there is a broad peak at 2θ = 22.5° for p-CQDs and 20.6° for x-CQDs, respectively. It indicates that p-phenylenediamine undergoes carbonization during the solvent heat treatment to produce a graphitic structure of p-CQDs and x-CQDs [34].

The surface functional groups of p-CQDs were measured through FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 2B). The wide absorption peaks at 3322 cm−1 and 3430 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H groups [35]. The intense peaks at 1610 cm−1, 1545 cm−1 and 1503 cm−1 can be designated to asymmetric CC and CN stretching vibrations [36]. The mixed peaks at 1230–1400 cm−1 correspond to the aromatic nitrogen heterocycles and stretching vibrations of C–O groups [37]. As for x-CQDs, the disappeared sharp absorption peaks at 1235 cm−1 and 1164 cm−1 belong to the C–O stretching vibration, illustrating that there are less oxygen-containing functional groups. The characteristic peaks around 800–1000 cm−1 represent the presence of –COOH and –NH2 groups [38]. According to the aforementioned findings, p-CQDs contain many functional groups of –OH, –COOH, and –NH2. These functional groups not only help p-CQDs become more hydrophilic and stable in aqueous solution, but they also have the capacity to interact with metal ions.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to analyze the chemical composition and structure of p-CQDs (Fig. 3 ). Three peaks in the XPS spectra (Fig. 3A) may be attributed to C1s, N1s, and O1s at energies of 285.08 eV, 400.08 eV, and 532.08 eV, respectively [39]. Data analysis yielded that carbon accounted for 73.86% of p-CQDs, nitrogen for 15.97%, oxygen for 10.16% and the content of other hetero elements including Er is less than 0.01% in total, which means that Er3+ is not doped into p-CQDs.

As shown in Fig. 3B, there are two peaks in the C1s spectra at 284.48 eV and 286.08 eV, indicating the presence of carbon bonds: CC/C–C and C–N groups [35]. Three peaks at 398.78 eV, 399.98 eV, and 405.98 eV were seen in the N1s spectra of p-CQDs (Fig. 3C), which were ascribed to the C–N–C/CN, NN, and O–N groups, respectively [36]. Four peaks at 530.28 eV, 531.68 eV, 531.78 eV and 533.38 eV in O1s spectra were attributed to the C–O, CO, C–O–H and C–O–C groups (Fig. 3D) [38]. According to the above characteristics, the addition of Er(NO3)3 will not improve the properties of CQDs by generating the chemical bond of Er-CQDs. However, the formation of p-CQDs surface functional groups is indirectly affected by changing the system state in the synthesis process [40].

The UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra of p-CQDs and x-CQDs were shown in Fig. 4 . As can be seen in Fig. 4A, there is an obvious absorption peak at 250 nm in p-CQDs, which can be ascribed to the π = π* transition of CC bond [41,42]. The absorption center at 300 nm is mainly attributed to the characteristic absorption of benzene rings [19]. There is also a weak absorption band from 340 nm to 400 nm that can be indicated the presence of the energy trap stemmed from surface carboxylic groups (C–O or CO) [25,43]. As shown in Fig. 4B, the maximum emission peak of p-CQDs was observed at 448 nm with an excitation wavelength of 370 nm, which may be due to the emission traps of a large number of surface states formed by hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino functional groups [44]. Fig. 4B also shows that the emission peak remains essentially unchanged with the variation of excitation wavelength in range of 310–410 nm. It indicates that the particle size distribution of p-CQDs is relatively uniform [33].

Fig. 4C exhibits that x-CQD has a strong characteristic absorption peak at 300 nm belonging to phenylenediamine and a weak absorption band about 500 nm. The emission wavelengths of x-CQDs at excitation wavelengths of 420–560 nm were recorded in Fig. 4D. The maximum emission wavelength located at 600 nm was observed at the optimal excitation wavelength of 520 nm. The excitation wavelength independence of x-CQDs is caused by the low total amount of surface structural defects. This phenomenon is similar to that of CQDs reported by Jiang et al. [10].

As shown in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence emission peaks of the corresponding absorbance (<0.1) of both p-CQDs and QS were recorded separately to obtain the integrated area of emission peak. Fig. 5C depicts the quantum yield of x-CQDs measured with the same method except that the reference solution was changed to RhB.

Fig. 5B and D were created using the integrated area of the fluorescence emission peak as the vertical coordinate and the absorbance as the horizontal coordinate. According to the following formula:
Ф X
=
Ф
S T
(
k X
/
k
S T
)
(
η X
/
η
S T
)
2

The quantum yields of p-CQDs and x-CQDs were calculated to be 31.16% and 2.69%, respectively. Therefore, the p-CQDs gotten from Er(NO3)3 ethanol solution not only have a significant increase in quantum yield but also play a great advantage in detection.

The metal ions may drastically reduce the fluorescence of CQDs because of their strong affinity or quick electron transfer [45,46]. Hence, the effects of metal ions on the fluorescence properties of p-CQD and x-CQDs were studied, and the results were shown in Fig. 6 . The overlaid fluorescence spectra of 18 metal ions detected by x-CQDs are shown in Fig. 6A–(B). Almost all metal ions can quench the fluorescence of x-CQDs, so they are worthless as targeted fluorescence probes for metal ions detection.
It can be seen from Fig. 6C–(D), only Fe3+ efficiently quench the fluorescence of p-CQD and the other metal ions have little effect, which means that p-CQDs can be applied to detect Fe3+.

Fig. 6E described the results of the interference test. There was no obvious change in the coexistence of Fe3+ and other metal ions compared with Fe3+ alone. The effect of anions (SiO3 2−, Cl−, ClO4 −, H2PO4 −, SCN−, NO2 −, HCO3 −, OH−, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, NO3 −) on the fluorescence intensity of p-CQDs is evaluated, and the results was shown in Fig. 6F. The findings demonstrate that the presence of anions has little impact on the fluorescence of p-CQDs. According to the aforementioned characterization, Er(NO3)3 alters the surface structure distribution of p-CQDs when it is present in the solvent. The larger atomic radius and more vacant orbitals of Er3+ affect the surface structure of p-CQDs during the synthesis process. Thus, p-CQDs fluorescent probe are able to accurately identify Fe3+ in complex conditions.

As shown in Fig. 7 A, the fluorescence intensity of p-CQDs at 448 nm decreased gradually with the concentration increase of Fe3+ (0–84 μM), illustrating that Fe3+ can effectively quench the fluorescence of p-CQDs. The linear correlation (R 2 = 0.9928, Y = −0.01X + 0.92) between the fluorescence intensity and Fe3+ concentration in range of 9–78 μM was shown in Fig. 7B.

The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 0.21 μM based on three times the standard deviation rule (LOD = 3σ/k, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank p-CQDs-Fe3+ sample recorded (σ = 0.0007) and k is the slope of the calibration curve).

The fluorescence intensity noticeably decreases when Fe3+ concentration rises, which is caused by the transfer of energy and electrons on the surface of p-CQDs. Fe3+ can be coordinated by O, N atoms from the surface functional groups of p-CQDs to form large-size chelates. The original conjugation system is disrupted and the electron transfer channel is blocked, manifesting as the “turn-off” of the fluorescence. In addition, the large ionic radius and empty orbitals of Fe3+ increase the surface charge of p-CQDs [47,48], leading to aggregation and further weakening the system fluorescence.

The comparison with other recently developed sensors for Fe3+ is shown in Table 1 . CQDs prepared by hydrothermal and electrochemical methods are commonly used for the detection of Fe3+, both of which can reach the “μM” level. In this work, the fluorescence sensor for Fe3+ detection using the solvothermal method is developed at a high level of sensitivity. Based on this fluorescence quenching mechanism, p-CQDs can be further developed for two fluorescence-enhanced sensors.

As shown in Fig. 8 A, only Li+ can effectively “turn-on” the fluorescence of the p-CQDs-Fe3+ system (p-CQDs-Fe3+-Li+), while the other metal ions have less effects. To study the stability of the fluorescence of the p-CQDs-Fe3+-Li+ system, the disturbing metal ions were added to the system. It can be seen from Fig. 8B, the effect of other metal ions on the fluorescence is negligible. As a result, the p-CQDs-Fe3+ based sensor that detects Li + has greater stability.

The effect of the amount of Li+ (0–270 μM) on the fluorescence recovery of the system is shown in Fig. 8C. It is evident that as the concentration of Li+ rises, the system's fluorescence intensity does as well. In range of 0–250 μM, the amplification of fluorescence is proportional to the concentration of Li+ (R 2 = 0.9939, Y = 0.0017X + 0.1124) as shown in Fig. 8D, and LOD was calculated to be 1.32 μM. As can be seen in Table 2 , Li+ detection is currently limited to three complicated and costly methods such as Solid state nuclear magnetic resonance, Mass spectrometry and Titration gas chromatography. The current study is a pioneer in Li+ detection employing the “off-on” feature of p-CQDs, which has the benefits of low LOD, high water solubility, low cost, and straightforward operation.

Chemical sensors for the content measurement of amino acid have been described using a variety of CQDs-metal ion quenching based devices [49]. Wu et al. used N, S-CQDs as a highly sensitive and selective “turn-off-on” probe for the determination of Fe3+ or L-Cys [50]. Zong et al. reported that L-Arg removes Fe3+ from the surface of CQDs and opens the blocked fluorescence emission channel, resulting in fluorescence “turn-on” [51]. In this work, an assembled p-CQDs-Fe3+ fluorescence-enhanced sensor for the quantitative detection of L-Arg was established based on a fluorescence “on-off-on” mechanism. Fig. 9 A indicates that adding L-Arg to the p-CQDs-Fe3+ system considerably increases the fluorescence of the combination (p-CQDs-Fe3+-L-Arg), but the other amino acids have no effect on the fluorescence.

To verify the stability of the fluorescence of the p-CQDs-Fe3+-L-Arg system, several related amino acids, such as L-Cys, L-His, L-Val and L-Lys, were added to the mixture again. Fig. 9B shows that four amino acids have no or less restorative effect on fluorescence of the p-CQDs-Fe3+-L-Arg system. The results of selectivity and interference tests demonstrate that the p-CQDs-Fe3+ fluorescent probe is able to accurately identify L-Arg under complicated conditions.

As shown in Fig. 9C, the fluorescence intensity of p-CQDs-Fe3+ at 450 nm increases gradually with the increase of L-Arg concentration (0–125 μM), indicating that L-Arg can effectively recover the fluorescence of the p-CQDs-Fe3+. Fig. 9D shows the effect of the amount of L-Arg on the fluorescence recovery of the system. It can be seen that the increase in fluorescence was proportional to the L-Arg concentration ranging from 0 to 75 μM (R 2 = 0.9721, Y = 0.008X + 0.067) with LOD being 0.12 μM.

Comparison of the methods reported in the literatures for the detection of L-Arg with the present work is listed in Table 3 . Despite low LOD, the other techniques frequently involve noble metal doping, resulting in high costs. This work is based on the p-CQDs-Fe3+ sensor, and the detection of L-Arg is achieved by the system fluorescence “turn-on”, which offers the benefits of high biocompatibility, non-toxicity, and cheap cost.

The interaction of metal ions with the surface of CQDs is thought to modify the surface defect state of CQDs, causing fluorescence “turn-off” [52]. Since the mechanism on “off-on” fluorescence of p-CQDs is still unknown, various characterization analyses were carried out to better understand this process. There are two distinct types of fluorescence recovery in p-CQDs-Fe3+ systems and that the fluorescence quenching of CQDs systems is driven by a shift in surface charge [53]. Fig. 10 demonstrates the zeta potential spectra of four CQDs solution systems, including p-CQDs, p-CQDs-Fe3+, p-CQDs-Fe3+-L-Arg and p-CQDs-Fe3+-Li+, respectively.

The results revealed that the change in zeta potential on the surface of CQDs was a significant factor influencing the system's fluorescence intensity. This occurs as a result of the modest positive charge present on the surface of p-CQDs (5.106 mV). The fluorescence “turn-off” of p-CQDs is caused by the binding of positively charged Fe3+ to its surface functional groups [54]. The surface charge of the p-CQDs-Fe3+ system rises sharply to 29.573 mV. L-Arg can rapidly restore to the fluorescence of the p-CQDs-Fe3+ system, most likely due to the guanidine group of L-Arg can coordinate with Fe3+ causing Fe3+ ions to fall off the surface of p-CQDs. However, since arginine solution can enhance the conductivity of the system, the surface charge of CQDs is not significantly reduced (zeta potential fell from 29.573 mV to 27.084 mV). The addition of Li+ to the p-CQDs-Fe3+ system was thought to disrupt the electric field force between Fe3+ and p-CQDs. Due to the small size, Li+ may enter the inner part of p-CQDs and open the photon transport channel. As a result, the negatively charged groups on the CQDs' surface were revealed, the zeta potential immediately dropped from 29.573 mV to −1.768 mV, and the fluorescence “turn-on”.

In summary, the special p-phenylenediamine carbon quantum dots with quantum yield of 31.16% were obtained using one-pot solvothermal method. The p-CQDs have an amorphous structure, a lot of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface and “turn-on” blue fluorescence. It has been employed with effectiveness for selective “on-off” detection of Fe3+ and “on-off-on” identification of L-Arg and Li+. Through intermolecular binding, Fe3+ can close the special fluorescence channel of p-CQDs surface and quench the fluorescence of p-CQDs. It has been used to detect Fe3+ in the range of 0–78 μM with a detection limit of 0.21 μM. By adding L-Arg or Li+, the fluorescence channel blocked by Fe3+ on the surface can be opened, which shows the fluorescence recovery of the p-CQDs-Fe3+ system. Based on the fluorescence “on-off-on” mechanism, this work achieves the detection of Li+ in the range of 0–250 μM and L-Arg in the range of 0–75 μM. The limits of detection were calculated to be 1.32 μM for Li+ and 0.12 μM for L-Arg.

Qian Wang: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Zixuan Pu: Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis. Ying Wang: Software, Validation, Visualization. Mingtian Li: Writing – review & editing, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Innovation Fund of Postgraduate, Sichuan University of Science & Engineering (No. y2021006 and S202110622040), Science and Technology Fund of Sichuan Province (No. 2020ZHCG0081), for which all the authors are grateful.
